skill.name: perigon-backend
skill.description: Apply Perigon backend conventions for ASP.NET Core, EF Core, Aspire hosting, DTOs, managers, controllers, and migrations.
skill.summary: Guides backend changes using Perigon ASP.NET Core, EF Core, and Aspire project conventions.
skill.author: AterDev

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported weak cryptography and system reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are false positives in instructional Markdown about ASP.NET Core, Guid v7 identifiers, EF migrations, managers, controllers, and Aspire setup. No executable code, network behavior, credential access, or prompt injection text was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 3, but the line is only the skill description for ASP.NET Core and EF Core conventions. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains descriptive metadata only. There is no code, algorithm selection, or security-sensitive operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 10, but the line describes entity modeling conventions such as Guid v7 identifiers, string lengths, decimal precision, enums, and date types. No weak cryptographic use is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is architectural guidance for data models. It does not invoke hashing, encryption, key handling, or random generation APIs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 10, but the text discusses entity fields and data types. It does not instruct the model to enumerate the host, users, processes, or network state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is domain modeling guidance. No reconnaissance command, host inspection, or data collection behavior appears there.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 11, but the text describes database choices, foreign keys, migrations, and file placement. It does not request system discovery or sensitive host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line references project migration files and database design. No command execution or reconnaissance target is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 13, but the text defines manager-layer conventions and common method names. It does not direct collection of machine, account, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited methods are application data operations within a backend architecture. They are not operating system discovery commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 15, but the text covers controller naming, HTTP response types, authorization, validation, and error handling. No reconnaissance behavior is requested.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is web API implementation guidance. It does not ask for probing, enumeration, or extraction of host information.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 16, but the text only notes Aspire hosting, Docker or Podman prerequisites, appsettings configuration, and editor diagnostics. It does not ask for unauthorized reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line mentions checking editor diagnostics and container runtime availability as development workflow guidance. This is low risk and lacks malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Pattern Dismissed: Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptography detections are false positives in non-executable Markdown. No cryptographic implementation was found in the reviewed file.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are documentation-only lines. They contain no encryption, hashing, cipher, or key-management code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Pattern Dismissed: System Reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The reconnaissance detections are false positives in backend convention guidance. No instruction asks for host enumeration, credential discovery, process listing, or network scanning.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited text describes source layout, EF Core usage, controller patterns, and development prerequisites. It does not include reconnaissance commands or sensitive data collection.

content.value_statement: Perigon backend projects need consistent structure across entities, DTOs, managers, controllers, EF Core, and Aspire hosting. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to follow the repository conventions during backend work.
actual_capabilities: Identifies where backend files belong across Definition, Modules, Services, and AppHost areas.
Guides entity design with EntityBase, Guid v7 identifiers, nullable reference types, string lengths, and decimal precision.
Applies EF Core conventions for DbContexts, migrations, projections, tracking behavior, and bulk operations.
Shapes manager classes with ManagerBase, dependency injection, business exceptions, and transaction helpers.
Shapes controllers with RestControllerBase, HTTP verb attributes, ActionResult responses, validation, and authorization handling.
Reminds agents to use Aspire and appsettings conventions while avoiding build or run commands unless requested.
limitations: Does not include runnable scripts, generators, or code templates.
Does not validate database schemas or run EF Core migrations by itself.
Assumes the target repository already follows the Perigon project layout.
Does not replace human review for architecture, security, or production data changes.

use_cases.title: Add a Backend Entity
use_cases.description: Create a new domain entity, DTO set, manager, and controller that match Perigon layering rules.

use_cases.title: Review a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: Check whether a backend change follows Perigon conventions for data access, managers, controllers, and migrations.

use_cases.title: Onboard to Perigon Architecture
use_cases.description: Learn where backend responsibilities belong before changing EF Core models, services, or Aspire configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Locate Backend Files
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner orientation before making a small change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the perigon-backend skill to explain where I should add a new backend feature in this repository. Focus on Definition, Modules, Services, and AppHost responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a New Entity
prompt_templates.scenario: Design an entity and related API surface before coding.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the perigon-backend skill to plan a new entity with DTOs, manager methods, controller actions, and EF Core migration notes. Follow Perigon naming and layering conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Review Backend Code
prompt_templates.scenario: Check a proposed backend change for convention drift.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the perigon-backend skill to review this backend change for Perigon conventions. Flag issues with entities, DbContext usage, managers, controllers, DTO mapping, and Aspire configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Complex Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced change touching data access, business logic, and API boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the perigon-backend skill to refactor this workflow into Perigon layers. Keep business logic in managers, controller concerns in controllers, and EF Core queries efficient and explicit.

output_examples.input: Add a Product feature to a Perigon backend.
output: Entity guidance covering EntityBase, Guid v7, required string lengths, decimal precision, and enum descriptions.
DTO placement for Detail, Add, Update, Item, and Filter models under the module models folder.
Manager and controller responsibilities separated by business logic, validation, authorization, and HTTP responses.

output_examples.input: Review a controller that calls DbContext directly.
output: A convention warning that business logic and data access should move into a manager.
A recommendation to keep controllers focused on routing, validation, authorization, and response handling.
Follow-up notes for projections, AsNoTracking usage, and error handling with Problem or NotFound.

output_examples.input: Prepare migration guidance for a schema change.
output: A reminder to use Code First conventions and place migration files under the Definition EntityFramework migrations area.
Database notes for SQL Server or PostgreSQL, foreign keys within a domain, and avoiding string-delimited lists.
A caution to avoid running build or migration commands unless the user requests them.

best_practices: Ask the agent to inspect the existing module layout before adding files.
Keep managers responsible for business logic and controllers responsible for HTTP concerns.
Request explicit EF Core query choices, including projections, tracking behavior, and transaction boundaries.
anti_patterns: Do not place business logic directly in controllers.
Do not add string-delimited lists when arrays, JSON, or related tables fit better.
Do not ask the agent to run build, migration, or service commands unless you intend that action.

faq.question: What projects does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports Perigon-style ASP.NET Core backends using EF Core, Modules, Services, Definition, and Aspire AppHost conventions.

faq.question: Does this skill generate complete backend features?
faq.answer: It can guide feature implementation, but it provides conventions rather than a standalone generator.

faq.question: Can it run EF Core migrations?
faq.answer: No. It reminds agents about migration placement and scripts, but commands should run only when the user asks.

faq.question: Does it support SQL Server and PostgreSQL?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance names SQL Server for commercial deployments and PostgreSQL as a supported database choice.

faq.question: How does it handle API responses?
faq.answer: It guides controllers to return ActionResult values and use Problem or NotFound for errors.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file is documentation-only, and static security hits were false positives.
